preamble: The shift from voiced dental stop [d] to the voiceless palatal stop [tɕ] (or [c]) involves both palatalization and devoicing, typically occurring before high front vowels. This is a major process in Mandarin Chinese (Ming–Qing dynasties) and Slavic historical phonology, where dental stops become palatal affricates in pre-palatal environments.

languageExamples.language: Old Mandarin to Modern Mandarin Chinese

examples.from: Late Ming Mandarin: Old Mandarin *[d-] before [i], [ɥ]
examples.to: Modern Mandarin [tɕ] (jia, jing, jiong in pinyin)
examples.note: During the Qing dynasty (1644–1911), dental stops [d] underwent palatalization to [tɕ] before high front vowels and glides, merging with earlier palatal affricates. Example: Old *di → Modern jī [tɕi]
Dental-to-palatal palatalization in the Qing period merged dental and palatal series, creating the modern jia/jie/jiu initial set.
Early Slavic palatalization where dental stops devoiced and palatalizedbefore high front vowels.
In various Romance dialects, Latin dental stops underwent multi-stage palatalization, often through an intermediate [tɕ] stage before reaching modern outputs.

sources: Norman, J. (1988). Chinese. Cambridge University Press.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages. Oxford University Press.
Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction.
Schenker, A. M. (1995). The Dawn of Slavic.
